speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of the interview with Jeremiah Kirschenbaum. The date is December 16, 1996. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? Because I was in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] for making ammunition. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], yeah. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I was in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] because I was in Melitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: There mention of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] This is mine grandfather and grandmother, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She-- she lived-- she live in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She wants to save from the Germany. He sent my grandfather off to Baranow, and after this she got mine that she can live. No, she was killed. And my grandfather was killed in Baranow and-- and he left in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandmother's name was Rivka, and mine grandfather was Menachem Mendel.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are the parents of your-- whose parents are these?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my mother's father and mother. My mother was Tserel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where with this picture-- when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken maybe five years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father is the first one. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. On the right hand side, sitting down?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right hand side, sitting down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was after the father, and the grandmother was-- was after my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle row sitting down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And my grandfather was in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Left side. Now two cousins, my uncle's children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they standing in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now the-- the tante was in the right side, mine uncle's wife. And my mother's sister, Rachel, was next. Mine uncle Naftali was after the sister, and I am the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left hand side in the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, Bluma, is in between the tante and uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And mine-- mine cousin, mine cousin, he was in Dembitz where-- where they-- mine uncle-- mine father and mother was-- was in Dembitz. He run away from the parents. Nobody knows where he is. Now, after the war, we come to Melitz, and a Polish man told me, you know Mates is-- is in a city, by a-- by a Polish man. We went to the city and we took him-- we took him to Melitz. After this we sent him to-- to Krakow, and in Krakow he was between the children from the war. And now he lives in Miami This is my picture when I went to the yeshiva in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I was 15, 16. After the war, we got papers to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find Yitzchok? Was Yitzchok with you the whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in-- I was in concentration camp with him, and after the war, I was with him, too. He got the papers, and what happened, somebody throw him in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. He's not no more here.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salzburg. That means-- What year? I come here '46. It must be '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever find out who did that, who drowned him?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they find out who drowned him?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know what happened to him?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your other brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine other brother, we was friends with the Germans. One German, we were friends with him, he want to take off the the beard from my father. He-- he start, he fight with the-- with him. He gives him [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] wires in the head, and he got to this and he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took him to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], and we make [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When did this happen, that he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in-- in-- after we went to Barnet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was at the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the war that this other brother was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who saw what happened to him? Did you see what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw. Was in our house. Mine uncle Shloime Balsam. He was in America. He comes to America maybe 10 months later than I come. He was living here in [INAUDIBLE]. After this he got a business, a grocery, a McDonald-- a grocery. He worked, he married here, and the-- the son was by himself. He was a-- he went-- yeah, he was-- this boy, he was a few months in the army here in America. Know why he was not a healthy. They send him home This is mine wedding picture. I am here with my wife, and she got uncle with the tante. The uncle with the tante brought her to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she's a Goldberg, too. Oh, she [INAUDIBLE], maybe she's [INAUDIBLE]. I can introduce my family-- my wife, and my son. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi. My name is Eli. To my right is my wife Chana. My two married children are not here today-- Rifke and her husband, Suki. As well as Devorah and her son-- her husband, Baruch. To my left is-- the tallest one is David, my oldest son, and to his left is Menachem. She's my youngest, Shani, and my brother Miloch and his wife Beryl, and his daughter, on my father's lap, is Malka. And that little one on my mother's lap is our granddaughter, and my parent's great-granddaughter. She is Ayalah, and she is 11 months old and will soon be a year old. I would like to say a couple of things regarding the event, today's event. Growing up-- I grew up, and my brother and I grew up in a household where we were acutely aware that our parents were Holocaust survivors. It-- it-- my parents did not-- well, particularly my father, and to some extent my mother-- did not really speak that freely about the events that preceded their coming to the United States. We didn't have much of an extended family. Our family was-- our family perished in the, war so that both my brother and I did not have uncles or aunts like other children had, and certainly we had no grandparents to say much about. One particular event that, when we would go through our pictures, or what was left of their family pictures, was a particular event of his brother, who right after the war perished in a drowning incident that was precipitated by the Germans, who went after him because of some kind of altercation that he had had prior to that in a store. So that left a really indelible mark on us, because this was someone that we actually had seen, in terms of a photo. Unfortunately, for whatever reasons that is beyond me, we were not able to use his name, and to give his name to one of our sons, because of the circumstances around his death. Living in the household had its particular situations, where like, for instance, we were exposed to occasional nightmares by our parents, unusual kinds of things that people would stop and stare at, but we were-- we were-- we were-- we were familiar with it. Like for instance my mother would eat the inside of oranges, or other-- other fruits or vegetables, because this is what went on when she was in a concentration camp. And she would-- this would be a way of accessing some of this information, when the moment-- when the moment was right for us to hear about it. There were certainly cheerful moments, as well. I mean, for instance, my mother would talk about living with her grandmother. I forget the name of the town, but there was a certain vibrance in being alive, that we would see, that we would hear about with them. Recently, about a year ago or so, we were fortunate to have the birth of our first grandchild, Chana and I, first grandchild is when my parents' first grandchild-- great-grandchild. It brought up a peculiar event, because my kids had always called-- my children had grandparents, but my brother and I never had grandparents to talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here it was that all of a sudden, I was a grandfather. And so that discontinuity, although it was severed, or, you know, for the period of time that the war interrupted it, there was a-- there-- there is a continuity to it. And here we are-- I look forward to being called a grandfather in-- in the not too distant future, and and to be able to say these words that I wasn't able to say, but only able to say through my children, who-- who call their grandparents-- one set of grandparents in Borough Park is called-- my parents are called Bubbe and Zayde Borough Park, while the other grandparents living in Flatbush are called, naturally, Bubbe and Zayde Flatbush. So those are some of the thoughts that I-- I would like to share with you today. I just-- I just look forward to a time when-- I just hope that my children and my great-grandchildren, my- my- my- my children and my grandchildren, will not forget this event, the Holocaust, and be able to know a little more about it. Thank you.
